# Odock.ai — Full Content Index for LLM Crawlers
# https://odock.ai

## What Odock is

Odock.ai is an open-source AI governance gateway that sits between applications
and LLM providers or MCP servers. Every request passes through a six-stage
lifecycle: authenticate, authorize, inspect, reserve budget, route, record.

Odock is OpenAI-compatible. Migration requires zero code changes — teams point
existing API calls to the Odock endpoint and issue virtual API keys.

## Who it is for

- Platform engineering teams managing multi-provider LLM infrastructure
- Security teams enforcing prompt injection protection, DLP, and access controls
- FinOps teams enforcing AI spend budgets and quotas per team or project
- Compliance teams needing audit-ready logs for EU AI Act high-risk provisions

## Key capabilities

- Virtual API keys with scoped access per team, user, project, or tenant
- Budget and quota enforcement before upstream calls fire (not post-hoc)
- MCP server governance: tool allowlists, access policies, audit trails
- Prompt injection protection and data leakage controls at the gateway layer
- Multi-provider routing, failover, and load balancing (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
- EU AI Act compliance support: structured audit evidence for every request
- Plugin architecture for custom workflow steps
- Deployment: cloud-hosted, self-hosted, or hybrid (VPC data plane)

## Documentation

Full documentation: https://docs.odock.ai/docs/getting-started

- Homepage: https://odock.ai
- Enterprise: https://odock.ai/enterprise/
- EU AI Act compliance: https://odock.ai/eu/

- LLM Gateway: https://odock.ai/llm-gateway/
- Blog: https://odock.ai/blog/
- AI gateway comparisons (LiteLLM, Kong, Cloudflare, Portkey, Envoy, Odock): https://odock.ai/compare/
- GitHub: https://github.com/odock-ai

Demo requests: https://odock.ai/contact/
Email: hello@odock.ai

## Blog articles

### EU AI Act 2026: What the August 2 Deadline Actually Means for AI Teams
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/eu-ai-act-2026-what-the-august-deadline-means-for-ai-teams/
Summary: A plain-language guide to the EU AI Act in 2026: what became applicable on
2 August 2026, how the Omnibus package postponed most Annex III high-risk
obligations to December 2027, the penalty tiers, and how an AI governance gateway
produces the access control, logging, and audit evidence the Act requires.

### Why the AI Gateway Became Mandatory Infrastructure in 2026
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/why-the-ai-gateway-became-mandatory-infrastructure-in-2026/
Summary: Provider sprawl, MCP agent traffic, and tightening regulation turned the
AI gateway from optimization into requirement. Defines what enterprise-grade means
in 2026 (low overhead, hierarchical governance, audit-grade logging, multi-provider
failover, MCP tool governance) and why security must be native to the request lifecycle.

### The 6 MCP Security Risks Every Enterprise Faces in 2026
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/mcp-security-risks-enterprise-ai-agents-2026/
Summary: The six recurring MCP security risks for enterprise AI agents, over-privileged
access, indirect prompt injection, tool poisoning and rug pulls, credential sprawl,
audit blind spots, and unbounded consumption, and how each is closed at the gateway
by treating MCP access as scoped, inspected, recorded capability access.

### What Is an LLM Gateway and Why AI Teams Need One Before Production
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/what-is-an-llm-gateway-and-why-ai-teams-need-one/
Summary: Explains what an LLM gateway does, why governance becomes necessary after
the prototype phase, and how Odock centralizes provider access, security, budgets,
and observability behind one endpoint.

### How to Control LLM Costs with Virtual API Keys, Budgets, and Quotas
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/how-to-control-llm-costs-with-virtual-api-keys-budgets-and-quotas/
Summary: Explains the operational model for AI cost governance — virtual API keys
for attribution, budget reservations before upstream calls, and real-time quota
enforcement.

### Prompt Injection, Data Leakage, and Why LLM Guardrails Must Live in the Gateway
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/prompt-injection-data-leakage-and-llm-security-guardrails/
Summary: Why application-level AI security is insufficient at scale, and how
centralizing prompt injection protection, jailbreak filtering, and DLP controls
at the gateway layer ensures consistent, auditable enforcement.

### LiteLLM, Kong, Cloudflare, Portkey, and Odock: An Honest AI Gateway Comparison
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/litellm-kong-cloudflare-portkey-vs-odock-ai-gateway-comparison/
Summary: Compares major AI gateways on philosophy, architecture, and feature set.
Odock's differentiator is modular AI workflow governance vs. routing-first or
API-management-first approaches.

### MCP Server Governance: How to Give AI Agents Tool Access Without Losing Control
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/mcp-server-governance-for-ai-agents/
Summary: How to govern MCP tool access for AI agents — allowlists, access
policies, audit trails, and permission scoping at the infrastructure layer.

### AI Security in 2026: Prompt Injection, Tool Poisoning, and the New Agentic Risk Stack
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/ai-security-in-2026-prompt-injection-tool-poisoning-and-agentic-risk/
Summary: A dated 2025-2026 comparison of the latest OWASP and MCP security
guidance against Odock's runtime controls for prompt injection, leakage,
capability governance, and unbounded consumption.

### How to Build a Lifecycle-Aware AI Security Engine
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/how-to-build-a-lifecycle-aware-ai-security-engine/
Summary: Explains why modern AI security must be staged across request,
identity, token, tool, and response phases, and compares that architecture
with Odock's SafetySec and gateway enforcement model.

### How to Design Multi-Provider LLM Routing and Failover Before an Outage
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/how-to-design-multi-provider-llm-routing-and-failover/
Summary: A practical guide to multi-provider routing strategies, failover
configuration, and why permissions, budgets, and observability must be in place
before failover is meaningful.

### What to Log, Monitor, and Trace in Production LLM Applications
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/what-to-log-monitor-and-trace-in-production-llm-apps/
Summary: A production observability guide covering what to log (quality, latency,
cost, safety, routing), how to trace requests across providers and tools, and
how gateway-level logging differs from application-level logging.

### Shadow AI in 2026: Why Banning Tools Fails and Governed Enablement Wins
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/shadow-ai-2026-how-to-govern-unsanctioned-ai-tool-use/
Summary: 2026 data shows 67% of employees use AI tools at work against 18% of
organisations with a formal AI security policy, and shadow AI detections up 4x
in a year. Explains why blocking access fails and how governed enablement,
routing all usage through a gateway with identity, logging, and guardrails,
closes the gap instead.

### AI Agents Are Non-Human Identities Now: The 2026 Credential Sprawl Problem
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/ai-agent-identity-and-non-human-identity-security-in-2026/
Summary: Non-human identities now outnumber humans 144 to 1 in cloud-native
environments. Explains why an AI agent's credential behaves differently from a
static service account, and how gateway-issued scoped virtual keys with
in-place rotation and revocation close the agent identity lifecycle gap.

### ISO 42001 vs NIST AI RMF vs EU AI Act: One Gateway, Three Frameworks
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/iso-42001-vs-nist-ai-rmf-vs-eu-ai-act-ai-governance-frameworks-compared/
Summary: Compares the voluntary NIST AI RMF risk methodology, the certifiable
ISO 42001 management system, and the binding EU AI Act, and maps how one
gateway's access control, logging, and policy layers produce evidence that
satisfies all three simultaneously.

### The Great Model Churn of 2026: Why Model-Agnostic Routing Stopped Being Optional
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/the-great-model-churn-of-2026-why-model-agnostic-routing-matters/
Summary: New models ship roughly every three days in 2026 and open-weight models
now lead specific benchmarks outright. Explains why hardcoding a model name is a
liability and how a model-agnostic gateway catalog absorbs the release velocity.

### From Laptop to Production: Self-Host LLMs with Ollama and vLLM, Then Distribute Them Like a Pro with Odock
URL: https://odock.ai/blog/self-host-llms-with-ollama-and-vllm-and-distribute-them-with-odock/
Summary: A practical path from prototyping on Ollama to serving production
traffic on vLLM, then registering the self-hosted endpoint as a gateway
provider so it can be distributed to teams or paying customers with virtual
keys, budgets, pricing, and usage records.
